text:
Person1: Do you mind if I smoke, Mr. Garrison?
Person2: How could I?  Dave, as you know, President Kennedy was assassinated on Friday.  A man named Lee Harvey Oswald was arrested as a suspect and then was murdered yesterday by a man named Jack Ruby.  We've heard reports that Oswald spent the summer in New Orleans and we've been advised you knew Oswald pretty well.
Person1: That's not true.  I never met anybody named Oswald.  Anybody who told you that has to be crazy.
Person2: But you are aware, he served in your Civil Air Patrol unit when he was a teenager.
Person1: No... if he did, I don't remember him.  There were lots of kids in and out... y'know.
Person2: I'm sure you've seen this.  Perhaps you knew this man under another name?
Person1: No, I never saw him before in my life.
Person2: Well that must've been mistaken information we got.  Thanks for straightening it out for us.  There is one other matter that's come up, Dave.  We were told you took a trip to Texas shortly after the assassination of Friday.
Person1: Yeah, now that's true.  I drove to Houston.
Person2: What was so appealing about Houston?
Person1: I hadn't been there ice skating in many years, and I had a couple of young friends with me, and we decided we wanted to go ice skating.
Person2: Dave, may I ask why the urge to go ice skating in Texas happened to strike you during one of the most violent thunderstorms in recent memory?
Person1: Oh, it was just a spur of the moment thing...  the storm wasn't that bad.
Person2: I see.  And where did you drive?
Person1: We went straight to Houston, and then Saturday night we drove to Galveston and stayed over there.
Person2: Why Galveston?
Person1: No particular reason.  Just to go somewhere.
Person2: And then Sunday?
Person1: In the morning we went goose hunting. Then headed home, but I dropped the boys off to see some relatives and I stayed in Hammond.
Person2: Did you bag any geese on this trip?
Person1: I believe the boys got a couple.
Person2: But the boys told us they didn't get any.
Person1: Oh yes, well, come to think of it, they're right.  We got to where the geese were and there were thousands of them.  But you couldn't approach them.  They were a wise bunch of birds.
Person2: Your young friends also told us you had no weapons in the car.  Dave, isn't it a bit difficult to hunt for geese without a shotgun?
Person1: Yes, now I remember, Mr. Garrison. I'm sorry, I got confused.  We got out there near the geese and it was only then we realized we'd forgotten our shotguns.  Stupid, right?  So of course we didn't get any geese.
Person2: I see.  Dave thank you for your time.  I'm sorry it has to end inconveniently for you, but I'm going to have you detained for further questioning by the FBI.
Person1: Why?  What's wrong?
Person2: Dave, I find your story simply not believable.

Person1: You're not crazy. Don't...wait a minute, just let me explain.
Person2: You're gonna explain.
Person1: Yeah JOE Why there's a little man in my head?
Person1: Yeah.
Person2: Why he's argumentative?
Person1: Yes...Yes...I'll explain it all. Just put the gun down.

Person1: Where'd you say the showersand thingswere?
Person2: Heyyou're little, aren't you?
Person1: Where is the shower?
Person2: Your hair's cute like that. You should never comb it.
Person1: I'll find it myself.

Person1: Look, you make this little run for me, I'll buy you a new rig.
Person2: Last year, this was a new rig.
Person1: But it wasn't a Kenworth.

Person1: I go in alone.
Person2: You don't get the Mask code unless I go.

Person1: All right, how about Ringo? I mean ... he's very upset, you know ... and as far as your girlfriend, little Audrey's concerned, she's finished with men for the rest of her natural, and another thing ...
Person2: A harmless bit of fun, aah, none of you have any sense of humour left these days.
Person1: Oh, it's all right for you but those two girls were scared to death! Honest, Grandad, why? I mean, why do you do these things?
Person2: You're left-handed, aren't you, Paul?
Person1: Yeah ... so what?
Person2: Why do you always use your left hand?
Person1: Well, don't be daft, I've got to.
Person2: And I take a left-handed view of life, I've got to.

Person1: You could always make it up to her.
Person2: How?
Person1: When a girl runs out like that, she generally wants to be followed.
Person2: She's not a girl, Col --
Person1: Whatever you say.

Person1: Revenge is a dish best served cold... well, it's payback time, sport.  ... see that building? I bought into it ten years ago. It was my first real estate deal. I sold it a couple of years later and made an $800,000 dollar profit. It was better than sex. At that time I thought that was all tne money in the world...  Now, it's a day's pay ... I had a mole in Wildman's employ. Gave me half the picture, then he got fired...
Person2: I don't understand.
Person1: Wildman's in town. He just became an American citizen. Something big's about to go down. I want to know where he goes and who he sees. I want you, sport, to give me the missing half of the picture...
Person2: Follow him? Mr. Gekko I...  It's not what I do. I could lose my license. If the SEC found out, I could go to jail. It's inside information, isn't it?
Person1: Inside information. Oh you mean like when a father tells his son about a court ruling on an airline? Or someone overhears me saying I'm gonna buy Teldar Paper? Or the chairman of the board of XYZ suddenly knows it's time to blow out XYZ. You mean that?  I'm afraid sport, unless you got a father on the board of directors of another company, you and I are gonna have a hard time doing any business...

Person1: I hear you've got visitors.
Person2: Would you guys...
Person1: No time for any of that crap any more, lady... I'll rip your lungs out if you don't answer fast.

Person1: Don't flee from yourself.  If you have a quality, let it define you.  Cultivate it.  It is you.  By helping me, you will help yourse...
Person2: What!?  What are you talking about?  Why should I help you?
Person1: Because you want to.  You've always wanted to.  Look at your pictures.  Look at your sculptures.  Look at those tawdry representations and then ... Imagine.  Imagine a world of the body as canvas.  The body as clay.  Your will and mine as the brush and the knife. Oh, I have such sights to show you.

Person1: You were quick enough to get Tom's help when...
Person2: Yes, yes.  I know.  Right.  And if it had gone well for me tonight, maybe I'd be keeping quiet about all this...I grant you everything but give me this...he does personify everything you've been fighting against...And I'm in love with you.  How do you like that? -- I buried the lead.

Person1: We're going back to nature, you and I. I'm going to retrain you. I'm going to make you free again if I have to kill you doing it.
Person2: But I like being human now.

Person1: Well sure, look at it!  Young trophy wife, I mean, in the parlance of our times, owes money all over town, including to known pornographers-- and that's cool, that's cool-- but I'm saying, she needs money, and of course they're gonna say they didn't get it 'cause she wants more, man, she's gotta feed the monkey, I mean-- hasn't that ever occurred to you...? Sir?
Person2: No.  No Mr. Lebowski, that had not occurred to me.

Person1: I hear that! See, I knew she was never going to be a ranchest, but I wanted to try -- I thought maybe she'd give music lessons to the kids in town or at the school, maybe even recitals. My son would grow up here. Maybe have one or two more. I'd teach 'em what I could. They'd play with my brother's kids. All grow up together. And even if they all decided to go out into the world, they'd always know where home was -- cause we'd keep it for 'em...
Person2: That's very important to you, isn't it? Home.
Person1: Yeah, I think it is. And I don't mean everybody's got to be married, have kids -- It's more like, knowing where you're from, where you belong, what feeds you, where you can go no matter what happens... Knowing what you're supposed to be doing while you're here.
Person2: How did you find out all that?

Person1: Hey Lovey, come here! Lovey, I asked for a glass of wine and look what I got. Come on, sit down.
Person2: So that's where you are. I might of known it. No wonder you couldn't hear me.
Person1: You were asked to have a glass of wine. Sit down!
Person2: And be poisoned out here in the open?
Person1: Certainly not!

Person1: Says here you're getting Martin Weir for the part of Lovejoy.
Person2: Yeah, we're getting Martin.
Person1: No shit, come on. How you gonna do that?
Person2: I put a gun right here...  ...and I tell him, 'Sign the paper Marty or you are dead.' Like that.
Person1: I wonder, would that work?  You know who I see for Al Roxy? Harvey Keitel. The man could do it in his sleep.
Person2: Harvey Keitel. Yeah. Maybe. He was pretty good in the movie "Fingers".
Person1: I missed that one. Or, hey, you know who else? Morgan Freeman. You know Morgan?
Person2: Yeah, Morgan Freeman. But he's a colored guy.
Person1: So what? Where's it say in this script he's white? Color is what the part needs, man, somebody to do it has some style. The way it is now, Ronnie could do it, play himself, some cracked out asshole.  So whatta you think of the script?

Person1: ...You keep pouring out that suppressing fire, Barnes. I don't wanna be caught out there with my rear hanging out you hear me?
Person2: Don't tell me how to fight this darn war, 'Lias, you go crying to darn brigade on your time. Out here you belong to me. Now move.

Person1: You see, Karl, growing up, only knew that sex was wrong and that people who did it should be killed for it. He couldn't really read but, well, neither could his mother. But, his father made sure that his mother knew what the Bible said. And she made sure Karl knew. You know he slept in a hole in the ground under a toolshed, right?
Person2: I knew he slept in a toolshed.
Person1: His mother told him that he was their punishment. Hers and his father's; from God, for having sex--
Person2: Before they were married?
Person1: I don't think so. Just period, I think. She told him... God gave them the ugliest creation he could think of. Karl has an entire book -- a notebook. On every page it says "Franklin Chapter 1 Verse number 1." He wrote that a few years ago after he'd learned to write. His father's name was Franklin.
Person2: That's really strange. What does it mean?
Person1: One of his Daddy's Bible lessons I imagine. Y'all pull up a chair. I'll go out and talk to him.

Person1: Fine. All of you want to be angry? Be angry... but I'm the one who took the ball and that makes it mine.
Person2: No.
Person1: Give it to me, Sami.
Person2: No!

Person1: You're getting a workout, you and Captain Forrest, your first night.
Person2: It isn't always this rough?
Person1: Oh, no. We have dull stretches every week or so, thank God, when there's nothing to do after midnight.
Person2: They don't have to be dull. I mean if you and me put our minds together...
Person1: Our minds?
Person2: For a start. I just have a hunch... well, it isn't entirely a hunch...
Person1: You're an attractive man.
Person2: You have a certain modest charm yourself.
Person1: But I'm married.
Person2: Something else we have in common.
Person1: Very happily married.
Person2: Same here.
Person1: And absolutely determined to be faithful to my husband. Do we have that in common, Captain?
Person2: It's a matter of definition. Faithful in spirit, yes.
Person1: I don't make the distinction. But the sex urge is a powerful force. In women just as much as men.
Person2: Ayuh.
Person1: You'd think now, with only six weeks before they ship me back home, it would be easier. But it isn't.
Person2: Of course not.
Person1: It's terribly hard. Sometimes the temptation is just too much.
Person2: Then why not, as long as it wouldn't hurt anybody...?
Person1: But you've made me feel strong again, Captain. Hawkeye.  You helped me pull together when I needed it.

Person1: Maybe.  I should call Ben.
Person2: Then it could all come out, sooner or later.  Nobody should be in on this.  Just you and me.  Or, more correctly, I.

Person1: You mess with this guy -- get him to do your dirty work and you think you can take the money and dump me?
Person2: No baby, you got it wrong.
Person1: This road don't go to Globe, Grace -- where were you going to meet me?

Person1: Do you really believe your life is meaningless?
Person2: I don't know.  But as a scientist I have to consider that possibility.
Person1: And yet you're willing to die for this cause, the one thing that's given your life a sense of purpose. Don't you see the contradiction here -- ?
Person2: It's getting late...
Person1: What are you so afraid of, Ellie?

Person1: I've seen lots of people.  It's not a big thing for me.
Person2: Hand me a towel.  I'm getting out.
Person1: I'm coming in.
Person2: I don't want you to!
Person1: We're not gonna do anything.
Person2: Why are you so stupid?
Person1: Why are you so uptight?
Person2: I don't even...what do you mean?  I don't even know what that means.
Person1: It means I'm gonna shampoo my hair and stay out of your way.
Person2: Hand me a towel.  Hand me a towel.

Person1: I did something stupid. I had a courier job -- picking up a package from the airport. It turned out to be money -- so I kind of borrowed it to pay my rent and now these dudes are after me.
Person2: So pay them back and apologize.
Person1: These guys aren't the kind that'll take an apology. They're the kind that'll break my thumbs.
Person2: You think that story's gonna make me loan you money?
Person1: It's the truth. If you'd just loaned me the money last time this never would have happened.
Person2: Somehow this winds up being my fault? You always blame me.
Person1: Come on, I'm your biggest supporter.
Person2: My bra is my biggest supporter.
Person1: I just need a place to lay low for a few days. Come on, I know you hate me but you can't wanna see me at the bottom of the East River?

Person1: No, don't be silly. What-what do we need other people for?  You know, we should-we should just turn out the lights, you know, and play hide and seek or something.
Person2: Well, okay. Well, listen, I'm gonna get a cigarette, okay?
Person1: Yeah, grass, right? The illusion that it will make a white woman more like Billie Holiday.
Person2: Well, have you ever made love high?
Person1: Me, no. You... I-I-you know, if I have grass or alcohol or anything I get unbearably wonderful. I get too, too wonderful for words. You know, I don't-I don't know why you have to, uh, get high every time we make love.
Person2: It relaxes me.
Person1: Oh, you-you have to be artificially relaxed before we can go to bed?
Person2: Well, what's the difference, anyway?
Person1: Well, I'll give you a shot of sodium pentothal. You can sleep through it.
Person2: Oh, come on, look who's talking. You've been seeing a psychiatrist for fifteen years.  You should smoke some o' this. You'd be off the couch in no time.
Person1: Oh, come, you don't need that.

Person1: That was so money. It was like that "Jedi mind" shit.
Person2: That's what I'm telling you, baby. The babies love that stuff. They don't want all that sensitive shit. You start talking to them about puppy dogs and ice cream. They know what you want. What do you think? You think they don't?
Person1: I know. I know.
Person2: They know what you want, believe me. Pretending is just a waste of time. You're gonna take them there eventually anyway. Don't apologize for it.
Person1: I'm just trying to be a gentleman, show some respect...
Person2: Respect, my ass. They respect honesty. You see how they dress when they go out? They want to be noticed. You're just showing them it's working. You gotta get off this respect kick, baby. There ain't nothing wrong with letting them now that you're money and that you want to party.

Person1: Are you seeing somebody else, Nathan? I just have to know.
Person2: Of course not.
Person1: It would just be helpful to know.
Person2: No.
Person1: Because, you know, you seem so distant. And you work late every night. And we hardly ever have sex, and when we do, it's... I don't know. It feels different.
Person2: I'm just preoccupied.
Person1: Do you like my new look?
Person2: Yeah. It's nice. It's really good.
Person1: I'm trying, you know.  I'm trying to be what you want. I want to be what you want, Nathan. All I want is to be what you want.
Person2: Shh. It's okay.  It's okay, Lila. You're what I want. You know that. You're exactly what I want.
Person1: Really?
Person2: Sure. Of course.
Person1: Because I'm really trying, you know. Rosie says maybe only another two years of the elctrolysis.
Person2: That's great.
Person1: I've signed up for a ballet class. And look at my nails! A real girl!

Person1: I had a lunch break so I thought I'd come by and see you. Your teacher is a fox!  Why don't we discuss that after class. But the burning is totally normal.  Oh look. Class is over already.
Person2: I thought this was a 45-minute seminar.
Person1: Right. Right.

Person1: What do you mean you didn't play with the other children?
Person2: It was on account of the birthday party.
Person1: Because you didn't ask them?  I don't blame them for being angry. Why didn't you explain what happened?
Person2: They ran away.
Person1: Why didn't you run after them?
Person2: I did.  I came to an old dark house, and a voice called to me -- a lovely, sweet voice ---

Person1: Momma told ya not to speak to strangers, huh?  Hey!
Person2: Don't touch me.
Person1: I'm not gonna hurt you. I just wanna talk.

Person1: You're right. I remember Grandpierre looking through it. But there was nothing in it -- at least, nothing that the police thought was very important.
Person2: Can you remember anything at all?
Person1: Grandpierre asked me about an appointment Charles had -- on the day he was killed.
Person2: With whom? Where?
Person1: I think it only said where -- but I can't --
Person2: Think, Reggie, you've got to think -- it may be what we're looking for.
Person1: That money's not ours, Adam -- if we keep it, we'll be breaking the law.
Person2: Nonsense. We didn't steal it. There's no law against stealing stolen money.
Person1: Of course there is!
Person2: There is? Well, I can't say I think very much of a silly law like that. Think, Reggie -- please think -- what was written in Charles' notebook?
Person1: Well -- it was a place -- a street corner, I think. But I don't --  Hold it. I'm on.

Person1: But I bet you didn't say you were going to split on him.
Person2: It wasn't like that.
Person1: Don't lie to me. Don't treat me like that.

Person1: Come on, just sign the loan papers for me. You'd do it if you loved me.
Person2: Alice, tying love to financial obligations isn't fair or healthy. Love shouldn't be used as a manipulation tool.

Person1: Why did you share that old photo of me from college? I looked so awkward back then.
Person2: I thought it was a fun throwback! I didn't think you'd take it so seriously.
Person1: It's just... I've changed a lot since then and I'm self-conscious about my past.
Person2: Everyone has their awkward phases, Zane. I’m not perfect either. Remember those terrible haircuts I used to have?
Person1: True. Maybe I'm overreacting a bit. Just, let's check with each other before posting old memories, okay?
Person2: Agreed. I'm sorry.

Person1: Captain, I've been observing the nurses on your shift. But naturally your own opinion is more informed than mine.
Person2: I'm glad you feel that way, Major, because you see it's a team effort... doctors, nurses, enlisted men... and I feel responsible for my whole team, and I want you to know I'm satisfied with them.
Person1: All of them?
Person2: That's right. We work well together.
Person1: Major Burns is far from satisfied.
Person2: That don't surprise me. If you're a good observer, you must have observed by now that Frank Burns is a jerk.
Person1: On the contrary, I've observed he's not only a good technical surgeon, he's a good military surgeon. And that includes how a man dresses and how he bears himself and his sense of what it means to be an officer in the United States Army.
Person2: And his track record, that don't count? Look, honey, when you watch the two shifts try to notice which one does the most work with the least fuss.
Person1: I've noticed that both nurses and enlisted men address you as 'Hawkeye.'
Person2: It's my name. Maybe that sounds silly to you but...
Person1: That kind of familiarity is inconsistent with maximum efficiency in a military organization.
Person2: Okay, Major, honey.  I'm going to have a couple shots scotch and go to bed. I'd normally ask you to join me but obviously you're a female version of the routine Regular Army clown. And that turns me off, so just leave my outfit alone and we'll get along fine.  See you around the campus.
Person1: I wonder how a degenerated person like you could have reached a position of responsibility in the Army Medical Corps.
Person2: Sister, if I knew the answer to that I sure as hell wouldn't be here.

Person1: For the show. It's Halloween eve and time for our show.
Person2: Oh, you mean on TV.
Person1: No, no, no it's so much more special than that... you'll see, you'll be the first to ever see. I think this is something you'll really love.
Person2: Great.

Person1: It's just that, you know, I was thinking about how, in class, you're always telling us 'that writers make choices -- at least the good ones. And, don't get me wrong. I'm not saying the book isn't really great -- I mean, really great -- but at times it's, well, very detailed, you know, with the genealogies of everyone's horses and all the dental records and so on -- and I don't know, maybe I'm wrong, but it sort of reads, in places, like, well, actually, like...  ...you didn't make any choices at all. And I was wondering if it might not be different if, maybe, when you wrote, you weren't always... under the influence.
Person2: Uh huh. Well, thanks for the thought, but, as shocking as this may sound, I'm not the first writer to sip a little weed. And furthermore, it might interest you to know that one book I wrote, as you say, 'under the influence,' happened to win a little something called the PEN award which, by the way, I accepted 'under the influence.'

Person1: I'm scared.
Person2: Then why are you sitting here with half the lights off?
Person1: I don't know.
Person2: Well, come on, get your coat. We're going to pick up Paul.
Person1: I don't want to.
Person2: Look, Lindsey, I thought we understood each other...
Person1: I want to stay here and watch this.

Person1: You feel violated?
Person2: Not me... all my other my patients. I smelled tar and feathers on the way over here.
Person1: You deserve it.

Person1: You'll get used to killing. Just forget about that mortal coil. You'll become accustomed to things all too quickly.
Person2: Do you think so?

Person1: But what?
Person2: I want you to know how much I love you.  That you've given a meaning to my life that I had no right to expect, and that no one can ever take from me.
Person1: Daddy --
Person2: No -- I love you so much and I want you to promise me something.  I don't want you to ever worry about me.  If anything should happen, I'm going to be fine and everything's going to be all right.  -- And I have no regrets.

Person1: Something's wrong with you.
Person2: Nothing is wrong with me.  I know something's in there - and so do you.

Person1: Sorry I'm late, Sid. That back nine at Riverdale is really murder.
Person2: Sure, sure, it's a tough course. Well thanks for coming, kid. I thought the board room would be a swell place to chat undisturbed -- it seems we're having some security problems here at the Hud.
Person1: Ya don't say.
Person2: Mm. Ordinarily I wouldn't bother you with it, but -- this is embarrassing, kid -- it seems to concern you directly.
Person1: How's that, Sid?
Person2: It's not important in itself -- some elevator boy you fired came to me claiming you'd stolen the idea for the, uh, the hoop dingus from him --
Person1: Huh?! He -- no, I -- he's just -- maybe I was a little rough on the boy, ya see I --
Person2: Ah forget it, kid, ya don't have to explain to me. He's a little person. He's nothing. Like I say, ordinarily it would just be a nuisance. But it seems -- well, there was a spy in the company...

Person1: I don't know that -- neither do you.  This subject may have been an epileptic -- thrown a fit -- fallen out of bed -- cracked her skull and killed herself -- there is everything explained -- the bruise on her head --
Person2: I can't believe that.
Person1: Believe it or not.  It's best for you to pretend that you do.  After all, it was you who ordered this specimen, received it here, and paid for it.  That makes you a party to murder.

Person1: Women and the space program have come a long way, sweetheart.  But after the wedding, no more complicated computers for my little girl.
Person2: But, darling, they've offered me a chance to head up the computer analysis division for the Jupiter probe.
Person1: You're heading up the division in charge of babies for Mr. and Mrs. Simon Kurtz.

Person1: I thought you would be on your way to Vegas by now.  Is there something you wanted?
Person2: I wanted to talk.
Person1: I don't think we have anything to talk about.
Person2: What about us?
Person1: There is no us, remember?
Person2: Except I can't get you out of my head, Grace.
Person2: I've thought about you every second since I left.  I can still taste you on my lips.
Person1: Stop it.
Person2: Why?  Am I making you hot, or does the truth scare you?
Person1: Because I know you're full of shit.
Person2: I mean it, Grace.  I'm getting out of here, and I want to take you with me.
Person1: I thought you couldn't leave.  I thought you couldn't get your car.
Person2: I could if I had Jake's money.
Person1: Is that what changed your mind?  The money?
Person2: I don't give a damn about the money. I want you, and I want to get us out of this shithole.  There's only one way to do that.
Person1: Are you sure?  About me, I mean?
Person2: I came back for you; this morning I came back.  Before I even knew about the money. You're what I want.
Person2: The only reason I stormed off is because you sort of spooked me talking about Jake.  But I've had nothing but time to think about it. It keeps coming back to you and me and us getting the hell out of here. But we've got to get the money, baby.  We get the money, I get the car, then we get the hell out.
Person1: You said you couldn't kill anybody.
Person2: We don't have to kill him.  Just knock him out and tie him up 'till we get away.

Person1: You're too modest, Nixon.  You're as evil as I am.  We're both from poor families.  But others pay to feed the hunger in us.  In my case, millions of reactionaries.  In your case, millions of Vietnamese.
Person2: Civil war is always the cruelest kind of war.
Person1: The real war is in us.  History is a symptom of our disease.

Person1: Pete, you're through!
Person2: What do you mean -- through?
Person1: I mean I'm scratching your name off the ticket Tuesday and running Czernecki in your place. It's nothing personal. And, Pete -- it's the only way out. It's a sacrifice we all ought to be glad to make.
Person2: Fred!
Person1: Now, Pete! Please don't appeal to my Sentimental side.
Person2: Fred, I don't know what to say. A thing like this almost destroys a man's faith in human nature.
Person1: I wish you wouldn't talk like that, Pete.
Person2: Our families, Fred. I've always looked on Bessie as my own sister.
Person1: If there was any way out...

Person1: I've got what I need for my report. There is... there is one thing that bothers me though.
Person2: What?
Person1: It's not really my place, but it's not easy for me to set aside the private detective part of me either. See, I know a little about missing persons.  When kids run, they almost always leave a note.  It's guilt. They want to say goodbye.
Person2: There wasn't one.  The police looked.
Person1: Do you think the police did a good job?
Person2: I don't know.  I think so.
Person1: It is possible... and I know this isn't something you want to hear. Your daughter may have tried to hide a note where she thought you would eventually find it, but where she knew your husband would never find it.  She might have wanted to tell you something...
Person2: No.  You don't have any reason to think that...
Person1: If the police focused their search in her room, her belongings, well that'd be only natural, but they may have been looking in the wrong place.

Person1: Who the hell is taking the time to write letters, spreading this shit about me?

Person1: My Lady Cosgrove's relationship with me was a singular one.  Her life was passed in a series of crack-brained sort of alternation between love and hatred for me.  We would quarrel for a fortnight, then we should be friends for a month together sometimes.  One day, I was joking her, and asking her whether she would take the water again, whether she had found another lover, and so forth.  She suddenly burst out into tears, and, after a while, said to me.
Person2: Roderick, you know well enough that I have never loved but you!  Was I ever so wretched that a kind word from you did not make me happy? Ever so angry, but the least offer of good-will on your part did not bring me to your side?  Did I not give a sufficient proof of my affection for you in bestowing one of the finest fortunes of England upon you?  Have I repined or rebuked you for the way you have wasted it? No, I loved you too much and too fondly; I have always loved you. From the first moment I saw you, I saw your bad qualities, and trembled at your violence; but I could not help loving you.  I married you, though I knew I was sealing my own fate in doing so, and in spite of reason and duty.  What sacrifice do you want from me?  I am ready to make any, so you will but love me, or, if not, that at least, you will gently us me.

Person1: Well, if the Clock People give you any inside information on the end of the world, drop us a postcard.

The Clock People see calamitous earthquakes as the agent of change, and they may be right, since there are a hundred thousand earthquakes a year and major ones are long overdue. But there are far worse catastrophes coming... unless the human race can bring itself to abandon the goals and values of civilization, in other words, unless it can break the consumption habit -- and we are so conditioned to consuming as a way of life that for most of us life would have no meaning without the yearnings and rewards of progressive consumption. It isn't merely that our bad habits will cause global catastrophes, but that our operative political-economic philosophies have us in such a blind crab grip that they prevent us from preparing for the natural disasters that are not our fault. So the apocalyptic shit is going to hit the fan, all right, but there'll be some of us it'll miss. Little pockets of humanity. Like the Clock People. Like you two honeys, if you decide to accept my offer of a lease on Siwash Cave. There's almost no worldwide calamity -- famine, nuclear accident, plague, weather warfare or reduction of the ozone shield -- that you couldn't survive in that cave.

Saunders--whether you ever mean anything to him or not--
